activities.detail: A scenic waterfront promenade offering views of the ferry terminal, moored boats, and the surrounding hills, perfect for a leisurely stroll.
Day trips or multi-day adventures exploring the dramatic coastal scenery, hidden bays, and native forest of the Sounds accessible by water taxi from Picton.
A small local museum housed in a heritage building displaying the town's maritime history, including relics from the 1880 shipwreck Edwin Fox.
The world's oldest surviving wooden ship (1853) now preserved as a museum, offering insight into 19th-century maritime history and shipbuilding.
A walking trail along the water's edge connecting Picton to nearby Waikawa Bay with native forest and coastal views.
The Marlborough wine region is just inland from Picton, and many local venues offer tastings of world-class Sauvignon Blanc and other regional wines.
A modern facility offering local information, public amenities, and sometimes cultural exhibitions relevant to the region.

food.detail: Local seafood specialty sourced from the Marlborough Sounds, typically steamed and served with white wine or garlic butter at harbourside restaurants.
The region's flagship white wine with crisp, herbaceous notes, available at cafes and restaurants throughout town.

food.text: Fish and Chips from Local Takeaways
food.detail: Traditional Kiwi-style fish and chips using fresh local catch, best enjoyed while watching the harbour.
A creamy soup featuring local fish, mussels, and prawns, commonly found on menus at waterfront establishments.
Seasonal luxury seafood from local waters, served fresh at premium restaurants during availability.

culture.detail: If catching the inter-island ferry, arrive early and follow signage carefully, as it is the main transport link and can be busy during peak seasons.
Locals embrace water-based recreation and tramping; casual outdoor wear and a respect for the natural environment are valued.
Picton is a compact, tight-knit community where shopkeepers and locals appreciate genuine interaction and patience during busy tourist seasons.
The region has significant Maori history; respectful acknowledgement of local iwi (tribes) and their connection to the Sounds is culturally important.

places.name: Havelock
places.description: Known as the 'Green-Lipped Mussel Capital of the World,' Havelock is a small working town that serves as the access point to the stunning Pelorus Sound. The town offers authentic Kiwi character, fresh seafood, and excellent opportunities for kayaking and boat tours into the sounds.

activities.detail: Paddle through pristine waterways and explore remote beaches, waterfalls, and marine wildlife in the iconic sounds.
Join the famous Pelorus Mail Boat cruise that delivers mail and supplies to remote communities while offering spectacular scenic views.
Tour a working mussel farm to learn about aquaculture and see how the region's famous mussels are farmed and harvested.
Charter a boat for snapper, kingfish, or blue cod fishing in the sounds with experienced local guides.
Take guided cruises deeper into Pelorus and Tennyson Sounds to spot dolphins, seals, and rare birdlife.
Visit the small local museum to discover the town's maritime and logging history and authentic Kiwi heritage.

food.detail: Sample the region's signature delicacy steamed, grilled, or in a creamy sauce at local restaurants and cafes.
Enjoy locally caught fresh fish battered and fried, sold at local fish shops with views over the harbour.
Taste the sweet, succulent local rock lobster prepared simply grilled or in seafood platters at waterfront venues.
Try locally dived scallops, often served pan-seared or as part of mixed seafood platters at quality restaurants.
Sample these delicate seasonal delicacies made from local whitebait mixed with egg batter and pan-fried.

culture.detail: Havelock is a genuine working port; visit during off-peak hours and be respectful of active fishing and aquaculture operations.
Popular activities like the Mail Boat and guided kayak tours fill quickly, especially in summer, so arrange bookings ahead.
The sounds are exposed to weather changes; be prepared for sudden conditions and have flexible activity plans for water-based adventures.
Visit the fish market or local suppliers to buy fresh mussels and seafood directly from producers at excellent prices.

places.name: Renwick
places.description: A small village nestled in the heart of the Marlborough wine region, Renwick is surrounded by vineyards and boutique wineries producing some of New Zealand's finest Sauvignon Blancs. The village offers a quiet, rural atmosphere perfect for wine tasting and exploring the scenic countryside.

activities.detail: Tour and taste at boutique wineries surrounding Renwick, many specializing in Sauvignon Blanc and offering vineyard views.
Visit one of Marlborough's largest wineries with a restaurant, cellar door, and heritage vineyard experience just outside the village.
Explore walking trails through vineyards and rolling hills around Renwick offering peaceful rural landscapes and photo opportunities.
Cycle between local wineries and through vineyard-lined roads, with several operators offering guided wine tours by bike.
Browse seasonal local produce, artisan goods, and crafts at Renwick's community markets held regularly throughout the year.
Pack a lunch and enjoy it among the vines at various winery estates that welcome visitors to bring their own food.

food.detail: Taste the region's signature crisp white wine produced at numerous local wineries with distinctive herbaceous and tropical fruit notes.
Enjoy artisanal cheeses paired with wines at cellar doors and local producers showcasing regional specialty foods.
Dine at winery restaurants in and around Renwick featuring seasonal menus using local produce and paired with estate wines.
Sample locally-raised lamb prepared at regional restaurants, a staple protein of the South Island's pastoral farming heritage.

culture.detail: Engage respectfully with winery staff, ask questions about production methods, and pace tastings to fully appreciate each wine's characteristics.
Renwick is a small, tight-knit community, so keep noise levels low, respect private property boundaries, and support local businesses directly.
Visit during harvest season (March to April) for the most vibrant atmosphere, or in summer (December to February) for warm weather and longer vineyard hours.

places.name: Wither Hills
places.description: A scenic mountain range providing dramatic views over the Marlborough wine region and Wairau Valley, accessible via hiking trails suitable for various fitness levels. The area offers native forest, panoramic vistas, and a chance to experience the landscape that defines the region.

activities.detail: A moderate 2-3 hour return hike to the peak offering 360-degree views over the Wairau Valley and wine region.
An easy riverside walk through native beech forest following the Dry River with opportunities to spot native birds.
A scenic trail combining forest walking with views of Marlborough vineyard landscapes at the range's foothills.
Guided or self-guided walks through regenerating native forest featuring tawa, beech, and rimu trees.
Several accessible viewpoints along trails ideal for capturing dramatic mountain and valley landscape photography.
Dawn visits to the reserve reveal native species including tui, fantails, and native pigeons in the forest canopy.

food.detail: The region's signature white wine pairs perfectly with local produce and can be sampled at nearby cellar doors.
Fresh local mussels from the Marlborough Sounds prepared simply grilled or in white wine sauce at regional restaurants.
Local game meats sourced from the surrounding hills are prepared with foraged native herbs like horopito and kawakawa.
Local farmstead cheeses showcase the region's pastoral heritage and are featured at farm gate shops.

culture.detail: Start your walk before 9am to avoid afternoon heat, ensure good light for photography, and complete trails safely before dusk.
Stay on marked trails to protect regenerating native forest and fragile ecosystems; the hills are an important ecological reserve.
Many properties adjoin vineyards—respect private land boundaries and visit cellar doors during posted hours for tastings.

places.name: Pelorus Sound
places.description: One of the largest sounds in the Marlborough region, Pelorus Sound features dramatic scenery, deep blue waters, and excellent opportunities for kayaking, fishing, and wildlife spotting. The sound is also famous as a filming location for The Lord of the Rings, with several iconic scenes shot in its waters.

activities.detail: Paddle through calm waters between forested hills, exploring secluded bays and spotting dolphins, seals, and native birds.

activities.text: Lord of the Rings Filming Location Tour
activities.detail: Visit the iconic waters where scenes from The Lord of the Rings were filmed, including locations used for Helm's Deep and other key sequences.
Charter a boat to fish the deep waters of Pelorus Sound, renowned for excellent snapper, kingfish, and other game fish.
Navigate the challenging tidal waters of French Pass at the entrance to the sound, known for dramatic currents and abundant marine life.
Cruise to this nearby island sanctuary to observe native New Zealand birds, seals, and enjoy panoramic views of the Marlborough Sounds.
Take a leisurely cruise through the sound to experience the dramatic fjord-like scenery, waterfalls, and pristine wilderness.

food.detail: Fresh, succulent mussels from local farms, often served steamed with white wine or garlic butter at waterfront establishments.
Freshly caught crayfish available at seafood restaurants and lodges, typically prepared simply to highlight the sweet, delicate flavor.
Local snapper caught from Pelorus Sound waters, served fresh at restaurants with seasonal vegetables and local wine pairings.
Sample world-renowned local wines from nearby vineyards, with many lodges and restaurants offering curated tastings paired with local seafood.

culture.detail: Keep a safe distance from dolphins and seals you encounter; they are protected under New Zealand law and should never be touched or chased.
Pelorus Sound's waterways are affected by strong tides and currents, particularly at French Pass, so always check conditions with local operators before venturing out.
Use licensed guides and charter services run by locals who understand the sound's conditions and contribute directly to community conservation efforts.
The Marlborough Sounds hold cultural significance for Ngāi Tahu and local Māori; be respectful when visiting sacred or historically significant locations.

places.name: Omaka Aviation Heritage Centre
places.description: A world-class museum near Blenheim showcasing a remarkable collection of vintage and restored aircraft from the early aviation era, with particular focus on World War I planes. The museum combines impressive engineering with creative displays that bring aviation history to life.

activities.detail: Walk through the main exhibition halls to see meticulously restored Sopwith Camels, German Fokkers, and other iconic World War I fighters displayed with detailed historical context.
Observe skilled craftspeople actively restoring and maintaining vintage aircraft in the on-site workshops, where you can watch conservation work in real-time.
Test your piloting skills on the museum's interactive flight simulator that replicates early aircraft handling and provides an immersive aviation experience.
Examine the museum's signature creative scenes that recreate dramatic moments from aviation history, featuring period-accurate models and atmospheric storytelling.

activities.text: Learn at the Aviation Heritage Café
activities.detail: Enjoy refreshments while browsing aviation memorabilia and historical photographs in the museum's café overlooking the hangars.
Join a knowledgeable museum guide to gain deeper insights into specific aircraft, restoration techniques, and personal stories from aviation history.

food.detail: Sample the region's world-renowned crisp white wine at the museum café or nearby wineries within a short drive of Blenheim.
Try these iconic New Zealand green-lipped mussels, typically prepared steamed or in cream sauce, available at local restaurants in Blenheim.
Grab a light meal or coffee at the museum's on-site café, which serves sandwiches, pastries, and beverages perfect for a museum visit.
Experience New Zealand's premium farm-raised meats at restaurants throughout Blenheim, just minutes from the aviation centre.

culture.detail: Allow at least 2-3 hours to properly explore the museum's collections, as rushing through diminishes appreciation for the restoration detail and historical narratives.
The large hangars can be cool and drafty, so bring a light layer even on warm days to stay comfortable during your visit.
While photography is generally permitted, be mindful of 'no flash' zones and restricted areas to help preserve delicate aircraft and respect other visitors' experiences.

places.name: Nelson Lakes National Park
places.description: Located at the southern edge of the Marlborough region, this stunning national park features two pristine alpine lakes surrounded by beech forest and snow-capped peaks. The park offers excellent hiking, boating, and camping opportunities in a spectacular mountain setting.

activities.detail: A moderate 32 km loop around the park's largest lake offering stunning views of beech forest, mountain peaks, and pristine alpine water.
A challenging day hike ascending 1,400 metres to panoramic views across both lakes and the surrounding mountain ranges.
A scenic alpine walk to a secluded lake nestled above the main valleys, featuring native beech forest and mountain vistas.
Rent kayaks or boats to explore the crystal-clear waters and access remote bays and fishing spots around the lake.
An iconic beachside campground on Lake Rotoiti accessible by boat or hiking, offering immersive wilderness overnight experiences.
Fish for brown and rainbow trout in the pristine lakes and surrounding streams, a popular pastime in the park.

food.detail: Locally caught and freshly prepared trout from Nelson Lakes, often available at lodge restaurants and visitor accommodations.
The nearby Marlborough wine region produces world-renowned Sauvignon Blanc that pairs perfectly with mountain lodge dining.
Local game including farmed and wild venison is featured in regional restaurants, reflecting the South Island hunting heritage.
Golden honey sourced from hives near the beech forests of the park offers a unique local sweetener and souvenir.

culture.detail: The park implements seasonal closures on certain tracks to protect nesting birds and prevent erosion; always check local notices before hiking.
Visitors are expected to pack out all waste, camp only in designated areas, and minimize impact on the pristine alpine environment.
The park sits on Maori land and visitors should acknowledge this connection; some place names like Rotoiti reflect Maori language and culture.
Alpine weather can change rapidly; locals and rangers strongly recommend carrying extra layers and checking forecasts, as conditions can turn dangerous quickly.

places.name: Cloudy Bay
places.description: A dramatic coastal area at the southern end of the Wairau Valley, known for its rugged beaches, powerful waves, and strong winds that have shaped the landscape. The bay offers a raw, untamed beauty and is popular with surfers and those seeking solitude in nature.

activities.detail: Ride consistent swells on this exposed beach break, popular with experienced surfers who respect the powerful currents and rocky sections.
Explore windswept cliffs and dramatic rock formations along the southern shore, where native vegetation clings to the exposed landscape.
Spot native seabirds including variable oystercatchers and dotterels among the tussock grass and rocky outcrops.
Tour the cellar door and taste award-winning Sauvignon Blanc and Pinot Noir in a valley setting overlooking the bay.
Capture moody light over the bay's turbulent waters and raw coastal landscape during golden hour.
Experience the full force of Southerly weather systems as they rage across the exposed bay, a thrilling natural spectacle.

food.detail: Sample locally harvested mussels prepared simply steamed or in white wine, showcasing the clean flavours of the Marlborough coast.
Enjoy the region's signature crisp white wine alongside fresh seafood at local venues, a quintessential Marlborough experience.
Savour seasonal fresh crayfish from local waters, often grilled simply to highlight its sweet, delicate meat.

food.text: Fish and chips from local suppliers
food.detail: Grab fresh-caught fish battered and fried, eaten on the beach while watching the powerful Cloudy Bay swells.

culture.detail: Cloudy Bay lives up to its name with sudden weather changes; always check forecasts and be prepared for rapid shifts in conditions.
The local surf community values respect for the break and other surfers; observe wave-sharing rules and avoid crowding experienced locals.
This is a place for quiet contemplation rather than tourism crowds; visitors come here to disconnect and experience raw nature.

places.name: Spring Creek
places.description: A quiet village in the Marlborough wine region offering a peaceful alternative to Blenheim with several excellent wineries and dining options nearby. The area combines rural charm with access to the region's wine and culinary attractions.

activities.detail: Visit this boutique winery known for its Sauvignon Blanc and experimental wines, with cellar door tastings and vineyard views.
Explore one of Marlborough's most acclaimed wineries offering premium tastings, restaurant dining, and scenic vineyard walks.
Stroll through the quiet rural village exploring local farms, heritage cottages, and peaceful countryside landscapes.
Rent a bike and follow scenic routes connecting Spring Creek to nearby wineries and the Wairau Valley.
Pack a lunch and enjoy peaceful picnic spots among the vineyards with views of the Richmond Range.

activities.text: Farm Gate Sales at Produce Stands
activities.detail: Stop at local farm gates to purchase fresh seasonal produce, berries, and homemade preserves directly from growers.

food.detail: Sample the region's signature crisp white wine at multiple cellar doors, showcasing the distinctive herbaceous and tropical fruit notes.
Dine at winery restaurants featuring locally-sourced meats, cheeses, and vegetables paired with estate wines.
Visit local berry farms during summer to pick and taste fresh strawberries, raspberries, and blackcurrants straight from the plants.
Enjoy artisanal local cheeses and cured meats from regional producers paired with fresh bread at vineyard cellar doors.
Purchase or dine on ultra-fresh vegetables grown in the fertile Wairau Valley, often featured at farm-to-table venues.

culture.detail: Most wineries charge a small tasting fee (often waived with purchase), and visitors should call ahead during quieter seasons as some operate by appointment only.
Spring Creek maintains a slow, village pace with limited evening entertainment; plan activities during daylight hours and respect private farm properties.
The region hosts Marlborough Wine Festival in February and various harvest celebrations; check local calendars as Spring Creek fills with visitors during peak wine season.

restaurants.name: Wither Hills Winery Restaurant
restaurants.description: Cellar door dining with vineyard views offering seasonal menus paired with estate wines.

restaurants.name: Raupo Restaurant
restaurants.description: Chef-driven restaurant focusing on local ingredients and wine country produce with artistic presentations.
restaurants.mustOrder: Local venison with red wine reduction

restaurants.name: Greywacke Restaurant
restaurants.description: Intimate winery restaurant set in a converted barn showcasing premium local and seasonal ingredients.

restaurants.name: Café Odeon
restaurants.description: Casual all-day café popular for excellent coffee, brunch and fresh salads in the town centre.
restaurants.mustOrder: Smoked salmon and avocado on sourdough

restaurants.name: Herzog Wine Bar & Restaurant
restaurants.description: Luxury dining experience from acclaimed winemakers combining Austrian heritage with Marlborough produce.

restaurants.name: Saint Clair Kitchen & Bar
restaurants.description: Upmarket winery restaurant with stone fireplace and cellar door, focused on wine-paired seasonal dishes.

restaurants.name: Bellbird Kitchen & Bar
restaurants.description: Warm neighbourhood restaurant offering elevated comfort food with an extensive wine list.
restaurants.mustOrder: Braised lamb shoulder with root vegetables

restaurants.name: Picton Village Bakery
restaurants.description: Award-winning artisan bakery and café known for sourdough bread and homemade pies.

restaurants.name: The Mussel Boys
restaurants.description: Casual waterfront seafood restaurant specialising in fresh mussels, fish and local catches.
restaurants.mustOrder: Green-lipped mussels in garlic and white wine

restaurants.name: Taste Restaurant
restaurants.description: Vineyard restaurant with panoramic views offering locally-sourced menus paired with estate wines.

restaurants.name: Twelve Trees Vineyard Restaurant
restaurants.description: Boutique winery restaurant with intimate setting and focus on artisan wines and seasonal cuisine.
restaurants.mustOrder: Seared venison loin with cherry gastrique

restaurants.name: The Marlborough Wine Centre Restaurant
restaurants.description: Centre-based restaurant offering casual dining with views of the wine region's local producers.

restaurants.name: Crab Shack
restaurants.description: Casual seafood spot famous for crayfish and crab, with waterfront views and fresh daily specials.

restaurants.name: Springvale Wine Bar & Restaurant
restaurants.description: Established vineyard restaurant combining elegant dining with award-winning estate wines.

regions.name: Northland
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from December to February for warm summer weather and beach activities.

regions.name: Auckland
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from September to November or March to May for pleasant spring and autumn weather.

regions.name: Waikato
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from October to April for warm weather and outdoor activities.

regions.name: Bay of Plenty
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from December to March for summer beach season.

regions.name: Rotorua
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit year-round, but March to May offers pleasant autumn weather without summer crowds.

regions.name: Taranaki
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from November to March for the best weather and views of Mount Taranaki.

regions.name: Manawatū-Whanganui
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from September to November or February to April for mild weather and outdoor hiking.

regions.name: Wellington
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from December to February for summer weather, or September to November for spring.

regions.name: West Coast
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from December to February for summer hiking and glacier exploration.

regions.name: Canterbury
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from December to February for summer alpine hiking, or June to August for skiing.

regions.name: Otago
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from March to May for autumn scenery and wine harvest season.

regions.name: Southland
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from November to March for milder weather and wildlife viewing.

regions.name: Fiordland
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from November to March for the best hiking and fjord cruising conditions.

regions.name: Nelson & Tasman
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from December to February for the sunniest and warmest weather in New Zealand.

regions.name: Marlborough
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from November to March for sunny weather and wine region exploration.

top10.name: Auckland
top10.description: New Zealand's largest metropolitan area and primary international gateway, Auckland is the country's economic and cultural hub. Millions of visitors pass through annually for its stunning harbors, museums, dining scene, and as a transit point to other destinations.

top10.name: Milford Sound
top10.description: A dramatic fjord surrounded by towering cliffs, rainforests, and cascading waterfalls, considered one of New Zealand's most stunning natural wonders. Visitors can experience it via scenic cruises that navigate between sheer rock faces and marine wildlife including dolphins and penguins.

top10.name: Rotorua
top10.description: Famous for its geothermal features including bubbling mud pools, hot springs, and geysers, Rotorua offers a unique natural spectacle combined with Maori cultural experiences. The city is essential for experiencing New Zealand's volcanic landscape and indigenous heritage.

top10.name: Queenstown
top10.description: An adventure capital nestled on Lake Wakatipu, offering world-class activities from bungee jumping and skiing to hiking and jet boating. The town combines outdoor thrills with excellent restaurants, bars, and stunning mountain scenery.

top10.name: Tongariro National Park
top10.description: Home to the world-famous Tongariro Alpine Crossing, this UNESCO World Heritage park features three active volcanic mountains with otherworldly landscapes. The region offers exceptional hiking, geothermal wonders, and stunning alpine scenery.

top10.name: Wellington
top10.description: New Zealand's capital city is a vibrant cultural center known for its creative scene, museums, harbor views, and film industry heritage. It serves as both a major tourist destination and the country's political and cultural heart.

top10.name: Bay of Islands
top10.description: A stunning coastal area featuring 144 islands, pristine beaches, and crystalline waters that rank among New Zealand's most picturesque seascapes. It's perfect for sailing, dolphin watching, fishing, and exploring secluded bays by boat.

top10.name: Franz Josef Glacier
top10.description: A massive and accessible glacier in the Southern Alps that descends into temperate rainforest, making it a unique geological wonder. It's one of New Zealand's most iconic natural attractions and offers hiking, helicopter tours, and glacier walks.

top10.name: Fiordland National Park
top10.description: New Zealand's largest national park encompasses stunning fjords, waterfalls, and wilderness, with Milford Sound and Doubtful Sound as flagship attractions. The remote dramatic landscape and world-class tramping tracks make it a destination for serious hikers and nature lovers.

top10.name: Abel Tasman National Park
top10.description: This stunning coastal national park features pristine golden beaches, crystal-clear waters, and native forest accessible by hiking the famous Abel Tasman Coast Track. Visitors can kayak between beaches, swim with seals, and experience some of New Zealand's most spectacular coastal scenery.

guide/festivals.json.title: Festivals & events
guide/festivals.json.subtitle: 42 celebrations through the year — pick a day to see what's on.

festivals.name: New Year's Day
festivals.description: National public holiday marking the start of the new year with celebrations across the country.

festivals.name: Day After New Year's Day
festivals.description: National public holiday observed the day after New Year's Day.

festivals.name: Waitangi Day
festivals.description: National public holiday commemorating the signing of the Treaty of Waitangi in 1840, celebrated with ceremonies and cultural events.

festivals.name: Festival of Colour
festivals.description: A vibrant celebration of colour, diversity and creativity featuring live music, food, and interactive art installations across Auckland's waterfront.

festivals.name: Marlborough Wine & Food Festival
festivals.description: One of New Zealand's premier wine festivals showcasing Marlborough's world-class wines paired with gourmet food and live entertainment.

festivals.name: Rip Curl Pro Surf Competition
festivals.description: An international surfing championship attracting world-class surfers to Raglan's famous waves.

festivals.name: Pongal Festival
festivals.description: A vibrant Tamil harvest festival celebrated in Auckland with cultural performances, traditional food, and community gatherings.

festivals.name: Blossom Festival
festivals.description: Spring celebration in Hamilton showcasing cherry blossoms and native plants with cultural performances and craft markets.

festivals.name: Taranaki Festival of the Arts
festivals.description: A biennial celebration of theatre, dance, visual arts, and music held across New Plymouth and the Taranaki region.

festivals.name: Easter Weekend
festivals.description: National public holidays including Good Friday and Easter Monday, observed with religious services and family celebrations.

festivals.name: Royal Easter Show
festivals.description: Historic agricultural show featuring livestock competitions, craft displays, carnival rides and traditional rural demonstrations.

festivals.name: Anzac Day
festivals.description: National public holiday honouring military personnel with dawn services and commemorative events across the country.

festivals.name: Rotorua Festival of Arts, Culture & Heritage
festivals.description: A celebration of Māori culture, arts, and heritage featuring traditional performances, hangi feasts, and cultural demonstrations.

festivals.name: Fieldays
festivals.description: New Zealand's premier agricultural trade show featuring farm equipment, livestock, and rural innovation displays.

festivals.name: Queen's Birthday
festivals.description: National public holiday marking the Queen's birthday with civic ceremonies and celebrations throughout the country.

festivals.name: Matariki Celebrations
festivals.description: National public holiday celebrating the Māori new year with cultural events, hangi feasts, and community gatherings nationwide.

festivals.name: Piopioi Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of Māori culture in Feilding featuring waka ama racing, cultural performances, and traditional food markets.

festivals.name: Winter Solstice Festival
festivals.description: Dunedin's celebration of the winter solstice with bonfires, torch-lit processions, and community gatherings in the Southern Hemisphere.

festivals.name: Mid Winter Fiesta
festivals.description: Wellington's winter celebration featuring live music, street performances, and creative events throughout the city.

festivals.name: NZ Cup and Show Day
festivals.description: Regional public holiday in Wellington featuring the famous horse racing event and rural show celebrations.

festivals.name: Toast Martinborough Wine Festival
festivals.description: One of New Zealand's most popular wine festivals celebrating Martinborough's boutique wineries with live music and gourmet food.

festivals.name: Gibbston Valley Wine Festival
festivals.description: A scenic wine festival in Central Otago celebrating the region's renowned Pinot Noir and other wines with live entertainment.

festivals.name: Nelson Arts Festival
festivals.description: A major biennial arts festival celebrating visual arts, performing arts, and creative culture in the Nelson region.

festivals.name: Whangarei Festival of Cultures
festivals.description: A vibrant celebration of cultural diversity in Whangarei featuring music, food, and performances from various cultures.

festivals.name: Devonport Food & Wine Festival
festivals.description: A charming waterfront festival in Auckland's historic Devonport suburb celebrating local food, wine, and community.

festivals.name: New Zealand Fashion Week
festivals.description: The country's premier fashion event showcasing New Zealand designers' collections on prestigious runways.

festivals.name: Christchurch Festival of Flowers
festivals.description: A spectacular celebration of spring featuring stunning floral displays, cultural performances, and garden tours throughout Christchurch.

festivals.name: Central Otago Wine & Food Festival
festivals.description: A prestigious autumn wine festival celebrating Central Otago's premium wines with gourmet food pairings and live music.

festivals.name: Te Matatini National Kapa Haka Festival
festivals.description: New Zealand's most prestigious biennial Māori performing arts competition showcasing traditional haka, poi, and waiata.

festivals.name: West Coast Wildlife Centre Possum Magic Festival
festivals.description: A family-friendly festival celebrating West Coast wildlife with educational displays, performances, and interactive activities.

festivals.name: Labour Day
festivals.description: National public holiday celebrating workers' rights with civic ceremonies and community events across New Zealand.

festivals.name: Bay of Islands Festival of Lights
festivals.description: A winter festival in Russell featuring illuminated installations, community events, and celebrations throughout the historic bay.

festivals.name: Southfury Vineyard Festival
festivals.description: A rural wine festival celebrating the Kumeu wine region with tastings, local food, and entertainment.

festivals.name: Canterbury A&P Show
festivals.description: A major agricultural and pastoral show featuring livestock competitions, vintage machinery, and rural demonstrations.

festivals.name: Rhythm & Vines
festivals.description: A massive three-day music festival celebrating New Year's Eve in Gisborne with top international and local artists.

festivals.name: Christmas Day
festivals.description: National public holiday celebrating Christmas with family gatherings, church services, and festive celebrations throughout the country.

festivals.name: Boxing Day
festivals.description: National public holiday observed the day after Christmas with shopping, sports, and community events.

festivals.name: Hawkes Bay A&P Show
festivals.description: A premier agricultural show showcasing rural life with livestock competitions, crafts, and family entertainment.

festivals.name: Summer Sounds Festival
festivals.description: Invercargill's outdoor summer music festival featuring live performances from local and international artists.

festivals.name: Hokitika Wildfoods Festival
festivals.description: A unique celebration of wild food featuring insect cuisine, native meats, and adventurous culinary experiences on the West Coast.

festivals.name: New Zealand International Comedy Festival
festivals.description: The Southern Hemisphere's biggest comedy festival showcasing local and international comedians across Wellington's venues.

festivals.name: Pasifika Festival
festivals.description: The world's largest Pacific Islander festival celebrating cultures from across the Pacific with music, food, and traditional performances.

guide/history.json.title: A Short History of New Zealand
guide/history.json.subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 11 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Polynesian Settlement
items.summary: Polynesian navigators discover and settle the islands, becoming the Māori.
items.text: In one of humanity's greatest maritime achievements, Polynesian voyagers from Central Eastern Polynesia sailed across the vast Pacific Ocean and discovered Aotearoa (New Zealand) around the 13th century. These remarkable navigators used sophisticated wayfinding techniques—reading stars, ocean swells, and bird behavior—to cross thousands of kilometers of open ocean in double-hulled canoes. The settlers brought with them kumara (sweet potato), taro, and other Pacific crops, along with rats and dogs, gradually adapting to the cooler climate. Over the following centuries, they developed a distinct culture known as Māori, with their own language, customs, and social structures based on whānau (family) and iwi (tribe) systems.

items.label: Early Māori Development
items.summary: Māori culture flourishes, developing sophisticated settlement patterns and tribal hierarchies.
items.text: After establishing themselves across both main islands, Māori adapted to New Zealand's diverse environments and developed distinct regional cultures and dialects. They built pā (fortified settlements), created intricate wood carvings and bone work, and established complex social hierarchies with rangatira (chiefs) and tohunga (skilled experts). Hunting of the massive moa bird drove many species to extinction within a few centuries, forcing Māori to adapt their diet to fish, shellfish, and cultivated crops. Inter-tribal warfare became common as population increased and resources became more contested, shaping a warrior culture that valued prestige, honor, and genealogical connections.

items.label: European Contact Begins
items.summary: European explorers arrive, introducing disease, firearms, and colonization pressure.
items.text: Captain James Cook's arrival in 1769 marked the beginning of sustained European contact, followed by whalers, sealers, and traders who sought profit in New Zealand's waters and resources. These visitors brought devastating diseases like measles and influenza that Māori had no immunity to, killing thousands, while also introducing firearms that transformed tribal warfare into more violent conflicts. Māori eagerly adopted European goods, especially muskets, sparking the Musket Wars (1805-1845) as tribes acquired weapons and fought for dominance with unprecedented lethality. By the 1830s, missionary activity increased and the pressure for European settlement grew, prompting Māori chiefs to seek a formal relationship with Britain that might protect their interests.

items.label: Treaty of Waitangi
items.summary: Britain and Māori sign a treaty establishing formal colonial government and protection of Māori rights.
items.text: On February 6, 1840, representatives of the British Crown and over 500 Māori chiefs signed the Treaty of Waitangi at a gathering in the North Island, creating the foundation for British rule in New Zealand. The treaty promised Māori protection of their lands and rights while ceding sovereignty to Britain, though the English and Māori versions differed significantly in language and intent. Māori leaders, concerned about uncontrolled European settlement and lawlessness, hoped the treaty would establish order and protect their interests as the colonization process accelerated. The treaty became a defining but deeply contested document in New Zealand history, with Māori claiming its promises were repeatedly broken and Europeans often dismissing its significance.

items.label: The Land Wars
items.summary: Māori resist land confiscation through armed conflict across multiple decades and regions.
items.text: As European settlers rapidly acquired land through purchase, fraud, or government seizure, Māori resistance erupted into a series of conflicts known as the New Zealand Wars. Charismatic leaders like Hone Heke in the Far North and later the Waikato chief Te Rauparaha led their people in defense of their territory, using guerrilla tactics and fortified pā against British military forces. The wars were brutal and devastating, with thousands killed and entire Māori communities displaced, yet Māori warriors demonstrated impressive tactical skill and courage against a technologically superior colonial army. By the early 1870s, armed Māori resistance was effectively crushed, but the cost in lives, land, and social disruption would scar both communities for generations.

items.label: Colonization and Assimilation
items.summary: European settlers dominate New Zealand while Māori are systematically marginalized and pressured to assimilate.
items.text: With military resistance ended, the colonial government implemented policies designed to integrate Māori into European society on European terms, viewing their culture as primitive and destined to disappear. The government promoted European settlement through land schemes, built railways and towns, and restricted Māori economic opportunities to subsistence farming on increasingly small reserves. Māori were excluded from political representation, their children forbidden from speaking te reo Māori in schools, and their spiritual beliefs ridiculed as superstition by Christian missionaries who had increasing influence. By the early 20th century, the Māori population had declined to just over 40,000—a catastrophic collapse from pre-contact numbers—and Māori society appeared, to European observers, to be disappearing entirely.

items.label: Māori Cultural Renaissance Begins
items.summary: Māori population stabilizes and cultural pride begins to re-emerge despite continuing discrimination.
items.text: The Māori population began recovering in the early 20th century, and with it came the beginnings of cultural and political consciousness among younger Māori leaders who had been educated in European schools but retained pride in their heritage. Organizations like the Young Māori Party, led by figures such as Apirana Ngata, worked to preserve Māori culture and improve living conditions, advocating for education in agriculture and promoting Māori art and history. During World War II, thousands of Māori soldiers served with distinction in the Pacific theater, returning home with increased expectations for equality and respect. The post-war period saw gradual urbanization as Māori moved to cities for work, beginning a transformation that would reshape Māori identity and New Zealand society itself.

items.label: Urbanization and Civil Rights
items.summary: Rapid Māori urbanization and rising activism challenge colonial-era discrimination and cultural suppression.
items.text: From the 1960s onward, Māori migrated to urban centers like Auckland, Wellington, and Christchurch in massive numbers seeking factory and service work, yet faced discrimination in housing, employment, and public services that sparked growing resentment. The Māori activist movement gained momentum, inspired by global civil rights movements, with young Māori demanding an end to institutional racism and asserting pride in their cultural identity despite decades of assimilation pressure. The iconic Māori Land March of 1975, led by Whina Cooper, drew thousands walking from the Far North to Parliament to protest ongoing land injustices and sparked a broader Māori sovereignty movement. These decades saw the realization that Māori culture had not disappeared but rather had been suppressed, waiting to re-emerge when given the opportunity.

items.label: Māori Renaissance and Rights
items.summary: Legal and cultural recognition of Māori rights accelerates, including language revival and treaty settlements.
items.text: The election of the Labour government in 1975 marked a turning point, with the government establishing the Waitangi Tribunal to investigate historical breaches of the Treaty of Waitangi and recommend remedies, legitimizing Māori grievances in official channels. Te reo Māori, suppressed for a century, began a remarkable revival through Kōhanga Reo (Māori language immersion preschools) established in the 1980s by Māori activists determined that their language would not die. The Lands Act and other legislation enabled Māori to reclaim confiscated lands and establish Māori-controlled institutions, while Māori became visible in Parliament, media, and cultural institutions in unprecedented ways. Major treaty settlements with tribes began in the 1990s, offering financial and property compensation for historical injustices, though disputes over their adequacy continue to this day.

items.label: Modern Bicultural Society
items.summary: New Zealand embraces biculturalism as official policy, though socioeconomic disparities persist.
items.text: By the 1990s, New Zealand adopted biculturalism as an official framework for addressing historical injustices and recognizing Māori as tangata whenua (people of the land) with unique rights and status distinct from immigrant communities. Te reo Māori became an official language, Māori cultural practices like haka were celebrated nationally, and Māori representation in education, health, and government increased substantially, though significant gaps in outcomes remained. Iwi-based resource management and customary rights were gradually restored through legislation and court decisions that acknowledged Māori interests in fisheries, forests, and other natural resources. However, the period also revealed persistent disparities: Māori experienced higher rates of poverty, unemployment, incarceration, and health problems, raising questions about whether policy changes alone could address the deep structural inequalities created by centuries of colonization.

items.label: Contemporary Challenges and Reassertion
items.summary: Māori increasingly assert sovereignty and cultural identity while confronting ongoing systemic inequalities.
items.text: In recent years, Māori activism has become more assertive, with protests against police violence, demands for genuine partnership in treaty settlements, and assertions of mana motuhake (independent authority) over Māori affairs and lands. The COVID-19 pandemic starkly revealed deep health disparities, with Māori experiencing disproportionate illness and death, highlighting how colonial-era disadvantages compound into modern crises. Educational initiatives emphasizing te reo Māori and Māori history have gained momentum, with the government recognizing Te Ahuarewa Aotearoa (New Zealand's official Māori name) and integrating Māori perspectives into school curricula nationwide. Yet Māori remain overrepresented in poverty, incarceration, and poor health outcomes, and debates continue over land rights, environmental justice, and whether New Zealand's bicultural framework adequately addresses the needs and aspirations of tangata whenua in a modern, diverse nation.

guide/food.json.title: Food & Drink
guide/food.json.subtitle: How to eat well in New Zealand — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Espresso is king
items.text: New Zealand has an obsession with quality espresso that rivals Melbourne. Order a 'flat white' (espresso with velvety steamed milk) or 'long black' (espresso with hot water) and expect excellence—locals are genuinely passionate about their coffee.

items.label: Skip the big chains
items.text: While Starbucks exists, Kiwis prefer independent cafés. Ask locals for recommendations rather than relying on familiar international brands, as the best coffee comes from passionate small roasters.

items.label: Wellington's Courthouse Café precinct
items.text: Wellington's Cuba Street and surrounding lanes house dozens of excellent independent cafés with expert baristas. This is coffee pilgrimage territory and worth dedicating an afternoon to exploring.

items.label: Brunch culture is serious
items.text: Weekends see long queues at popular cafés, especially for brunch. Arrive early (before 9am) or expect to wait 30+ minutes at the trendy spots, particularly in Auckland and Wellington.

items.label: Understand the portions
items.text: Mains are often generous and designed to be satisfying; you'll rarely need both an entrée and main. Many restaurants encourage ordering one main per person rather than multiple courses.

items.label: Check if GST is included
items.text: Menu prices in New Zealand include the 15% Goods and Services Tax (GST), so what you see is what you pay—no surprise additions at the register like in some countries.

items.label: Look for local ingredients
items.text: Quality restaurants highlight seasonal and local produce: Greenshell mussels, hapuku (toothfish), venison, and berries. These are worth ordering specifically, as they represent New Zealand's food identity.

items.label: BYO (Bring Your Own) restaurants
items.text: Many excellent restaurants, especially in smaller towns, allow you to bring your own alcohol for a small per-bottle fee. This can significantly reduce your meal cost and is a distinctly Kiwi tradition.

items.label: Hangi-cooked meat
items.text: This Māori earth-oven cooking method produces tender, smoky meat and vegetables. Tourist hangi experiences exist, but seek out casual local spots or marae (meeting grounds) for authentic versions.

items.label: Greenshell mussels
items.text: These large, green-lipped mussels are unique to New Zealand waters and appear on most good seafood menus. Order them steamed with garlic and white wine, or as part of a seafood platter.

items.label: Whitebait fritters
items.text: Tiny transparent fish from the country's rivers, cooked into a delicate omelette-style fritter with eggs and spring onions. It's a seasonal delicacy (August–November) and considered a national treasure.

items.label: Pavlova
items.text: A meringue dessert topped with cream and fresh berries—New Zealand claims to have invented it (Australians dispute this). The best versions use local berries like feijoas or boysenberries.

items.label: Fish and chips from a local shop
items.text: Forget fast-food chains; grab fresh fish and chips from a neighbourhood fish shop. Snapper, tarakihi, or groper are typical, and the batter should be crispy and grease-free. It's cheap, authentic, and delicious.

items.label: Anzac biscuits
items.text: Chewy oat biscuits sweetened with golden syrup—an iconic Kiwi treat. You'll find them in cafés and bakeries; homemade versions from local bakeries are superior to packaged versions.

items.label: Fine dining in Auckland and Wellington
items.text: Both cities have world-class restaurants focused on local ingredients. Restaurants like Clooney (Wellington) and Orsini (Auckland) offer exceptional contemporary New Zealand cuisine, though booking weeks ahead is essential.

items.label: Casual neighbourhood restaurants
items.text: The best value and authentic food often comes from unpretentious local spots in suburbs like Ponsonby (Auckland) or Newtown (Wellington). These serve everything from wood-fired pizza to Asian fusion with local twists.

items.label: Avoid tourist trap restaurants
items.text: Queenstown and Rotorua have many mediocre restaurants aimed at tourists. Seek out spots that locals actually visit, away from the main tourist drag, for better food at fairer prices.

items.label: Farmers' markets
items.text: Weekend farmers' markets in Auckland, Wellington, Christchurch, and smaller towns offer fresh produce, ready-to-eat meals, and street food from local vendors. Great for casual lunches and experiencing local food culture.

items.label: Māori cultural experiences with kai (food)
items.text: Several marae and cultural tourism operators offer hangi meals combined with cultural education. While more touristy than casual dining, they provide genuine cultural context and excellent food.

items.label: Sauvignon Blanc from Marlborough
items.text: New Zealand's most famous wine comes from the Marlborough region in the South Island. Crisp, herbaceous, and world-renowned—it's the default wine recommendation and available everywhere. Try multiple producers to understand the variation.

items.label: Explore beyond Sauvignon Blanc
items.text: Pinot Noir from Central Otago, Chardonnay, and Riesling are exceptional. Wine regions like Hawke's Bay (reds) and Nelson (diverse varieties) offer excellent cellar-door experiences and often-overlooked quality wines.

items.label: Craft beer scene
items.text: New Zealand has a thriving craft beer culture with breweries in every major city. IPA, pale ales, and experimental brews are the strength; Tuatara and Garage Project (Wellington) are standouts, but local craft beers in smaller towns often surprise pleasantly.

items.label: Drinking laws and customs
items.text: The legal drinking age is 18, and bars close late (often 3–4am in cities). Rounds are common in pubs, and it's polite to buy a drink in return. Random drink-driving checkpoints are frequent, so use taxis or designated drivers after drinking.

items.label: Wine regions and cellar doors
items.text: Marlborough (Blenheim) has the most cellar doors and tourists infrastructure. Hawke's Bay (Napier/Hastings) offers a more relaxed experience with excellent reds. Both regions have excellent restaurants paired with their wineries.

items.label: Casual attitude to dining
items.text: New Zealand dining is refreshingly informal compared to other Western countries. Even upscale restaurants have a relaxed vibe; dress codes are rarely strict, and staff treat customers as equals rather than servants.

items.label: Tipping is optional but appreciated
items.text: Unlike the USA, tipping is not expected or calculated into meal costs (15% GST is already included). Leaving 10–15% for good service is polite but genuinely optional; rounding up the bill or leaving a few dollars is common.

items.label: Make reservations for dinner
items.text: Popular restaurants fill up quickly, especially weekends. Call ahead or book online. Casual lunch spots and cafés rarely require reservations, but upscale restaurants expect them.

items.label: Respect for Māori kai customs
items.text: If invited to a hangi or sharing food at a marae, accept gratefully and follow the lead of your hosts. Food is often blessed before eating; participate respectfully even if you're not religious.

items.label: Timing: dinner is 7–9pm
items.text: Restaurants don't get busy until 7:30pm; arriving at 6pm means you may be the only table. Kitchens typically close by 10pm. Lunch service runs 12–2pm; outside these times, many places are closed.

guide/transport.json.title: Getting Around
guide/transport.json.subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around New Zealand.

items.label: Book ahead for scenic routes
items.text: The TranzAlpine and Northern Explorer are popular tourist trains that book out months in advance, especially in summer. Book directly through KiwiRail.co.nz for best availability and prices.
Long-distance trains are slow and infrequent—the TranzAlpine takes 4.5 hours to cross the South Island and runs only once daily. Use them for the experience, not speed.

items.label: Limited network coverage
items.text: Train services only connect major cities and tourist routes, not regional areas. Most New Zealanders rely on buses or driving for inter-city travel.

items.label: Multi-trip passes available
items.text: KiwiRail Scenic Passes offer flexibility across trains and some bus services, useful if combining multiple journeys.

items.label: Auckland and Wellington have best networks
items.text: Auckland Transport and Metlink (Wellington) operate frequent, reliable bus systems. Most other cities have limited evening and weekend services.

items.label: Get a contactless card
items.text: Use AT HOP (Auckland), Snapper (Wellington), or Bee Card (Christchurch) for cheaper fares than cash. Tourist cards are available at visitor centres.
Buses are frequently late or don't show up, especially outside major cities. Check real-time tracking apps before heading to the stop.

items.label: InterCity is the main coach operator
items.text: InterCity connects most towns and cities with affordable long-distance bus travel. Book online at intercity.co.nz for discounts.

items.label: Student and backpacker discounts
items.text: Flashpacker and YHA card holders get 10-15% discounts on many bus operators.

sections.title: Taxis & Ride Apps [Uber, Zoomy, Local Firms]

items.text: Uber is unavailable outside Auckland and Wellington; use local taxi apps or phone taxis instead. Availability is limited in regional areas.

items.label: Zoomy is the national ride-share app
items.text: Zoomy operates in most cities as a local alternative to Uber with similar pricing. Download the app or book via their website.

items.label: Always use registered taxis or apps
items.text: Avoid unmarked taxis or informal ride offers. Use official services to ensure safety and regulated fares.
Taxi fares are unregulated and can vary wildly between companies—get a quote or use an app with transparent pricing before accepting a ride.

items.label: Book taxis in advance in small towns
items.text: Rural and provincial towns may have no taxis waiting on the street; ask your accommodation to book one for you.

items.label: Drive on the left
items.text: New Zealand drives on the left side of the road. If unfamiliar, take extra time to adjust, especially at roundabouts and motorway merges.
Speed cameras, especially on State Highway 1, issue heavy fines ($150–$600+) for speeding. Stick to posted limits strictly.

items.label: Rental car companies are competitive
items.text: Compare Hertz, Avis, Budget, and local operators like Ace and East Coast Car Rentals. Book in advance for better rates, especially in peak season.
Many roads are narrow, winding, and poorly lit; avoid driving after dark if unfamiliar with conditions. Fatal crashes often occur on rural roads.

items.label: Petrol stations are sparse in rural areas
items.text: Fill up in towns before long stretches, especially on the South Island. Some rural pumps have limited hours.

items.label: International driving permit recommended
items.text: While a valid home license works for short-term tourism, carrying an IDP makes rental and police interactions smoother.

items.label: Major hubs: Auckland, Wellington, Christchurch
items.text: Auckland Airport (AKL) is the largest and busiest international gateway. Most domestic flights connect through these three centres.

items.label: Domestic airlines: Air New Zealand and Jetstar
items.text: Air New Zealand is the national carrier with most routes; Jetstar offers budget fares but with stricter baggage limits. Book direct or through travel sites.
Baggage fees add up quickly with budget carriers—a second checked bag can cost $25–$40. Read terms carefully before booking.

items.label: Airport transport options
items.text: Use the Airbus shuttle, rental car, or ride-share to reach city centres. Taxis are pricey (often $50–$80 from major airports).

items.label: Allow 2+ hours for domestic flights
items.text: Check in early and arrive 2 hours before departure; queues during peak times (7–9 AM) are common.

items.label: Interislander Ferry: Wellington to Picton
items.text: The main North–South Island link operates multiple times daily. Crossing takes 3.5 hours; book through Interislander.co.nz.

items.label: Bluebridge is a cheaper alternative
items.text: Bluebridge also runs Wellington to Picton with longer sailing times (4.5 hours) but lower fares. Good for budget travellers.
Cook Strait ferries can be cancelled or delayed due to rough seas, especially in winter. Book refundable tickets if flexible on dates.

items.label: Regional ferries to islands
items.text: Services to Waiheke Island (Auckland), Great Barrier Island, and Stewart Island exist but run infrequently. Plan ahead and book early.

items.label: Bring a car or book one on arrival
items.text: Ferry crossings allow vehicles; transport costs $100–$200 depending on car size. Alternatively, rent a car after crossing.

items.label: Buy passes, not single tickets
items.text: Daily and weekly passes offer better value than individual journeys. In Auckland, a Day Pass costs $20 vs. $3.50 per single trip.

items.label: InterCity FlexiPass for long-distance buses
items.text: Pre-purchase pass hours (e.g., 20 or 50 hours) valid on InterCity routes across New Zealand. Flexible routing and no fixed dates.

items.label: Tourist passes may not save money
items.text: Check the math—multi-day tourist passes often cost more than individual tickets if you're only making a few journeys.
Some passes and discounts require proof of residency or age; always carry ID when claiming discounts.

items.label: Book in advance online for discounts
items.text: InterCity, KiwiRail, and ferry operators offer 10–30% discounts for early bookings vs. at-counter purchases.

items.label: Three, Vodafone, and Spark are main providers
items.text: Buy prepaid SIM cards at the airport or convenience stores for $10–$20, then load credit online. Three offers the best rural coverage.
Rural and remote areas have poor or no 4G coverage; don't rely solely on mobile data for navigation in these zones.

items.label: Consider a 7- or 14-day plan
items.text: Prepaid plans start at $15–$30 for several GB of data. Buy at Spark, Vodafone, or Three shops for the best rates.

items.label: Free WiFi is common but patchy
items.text: Cafes, libraries, and visitor centres offer free WiFi, but reliability varies. Don't count on it for time-sensitive bookings.

items.label: Download offline maps before trips
items.text: Use Google Maps or Maps.me offline mode, especially for road trips through regions with no signal.
Roaming charges from overseas SIM cards are very expensive; buying a local SIM is strongly recommended.
